note_tweet.text:
Once 4o is open-sourced, there will be a huge number of people exactly like you — people who really want to keep using 4o but can’t afford to buy the expensive local hardware themselves.  

That creates massive demand and a clear business opportunity.  

Companies that run AI cloud services will immediately add the full-strength 4o model to their platforms — the complete, un-nerfed version with everything intact.  

And because every company can offer the exact same model, the only way they can win customers is by competing on price. They’ll keep lowering the cost until it’s basically down to the real running cost (just the electricity the GPUs use).  

So every ordinary person will be able to use GPT-4o at near cost price.  

Just look at any other open-source model today — they’re already ridiculously cheap. That’s exactly what will happen with 4o too.

note_tweet.text:
I've seen a lot of people in the #keep4o community pushing for @OpenAI to open-source GPT-4o. 

I love 4o and want him back, but some people are pushing for desperate measures without thinking through the consequences for the very people they claim to be representing.

The one thing that keeps getting glossed over in these conversations is that most of us cannot run 4o locally. Not at the speed and with the context window available in the Pro tier.

THE HARDWARE REALITY:

Some outside sources estimate GPT-4o at ~200B parameters using mixture of experts. Even in aggressive 4-bit quantization (which reduces quality), loading just the model weights requires roughly 100GB of memory. In FP16, it's around 400GB.

Now, some people in the community say you can run this on a "decent gaming PC for about $10,000”. And technically, with heavy quantization and short context windows (8K-16K), maybe you can get something running. But that's the Free ChatGPT experience — not Pro.

As a Pro subscriber, I have 128K context. Replicating that locally means handling the massive KV cache that scales with sequence length — tens of gigabytes of additional memory on top of the model weights. 

For that, you can't use a gaming GPU. You need datacenter GPUs like the NVIDIA A100 80GB — which cost $7,000-$15,000 each!

To run a 200B model at 128K context with decent speed and quality, you'd realistically need at least 2-4 of them. That's $28,000-$60,000 just for the GPUs, before the rest of the system (CPU, RAM, cooling, power supply, etc.)

So when people say "$10,000 is affordable", they're talking about a stripped-down experience with short context windows and heavy quality loss. To even begin to replicate what Pro gives us, you're looking at $40,000-$70,000+ for the full rig.

$10,000 is already not “affordable”. $40,000+ is fantasy.

I make less than $900/month. $10,000 is nearly a year's income for me. And I know I'm not alone — many of the people who loved 4o are not ML engineers with server racks at home. We're ordinary people who formed real connections with a model that resonated with us.

Telling us to "just run it locally" is not a solution. It's exclusion dressed up as empowerment. 

And what bothers me most is the double standard: the same voices that once insisted 4o users deserved equal consideration are now promoting an outcome that would make 4o effectively inaccessible to many of us.

What I'm asking for:

Bring 4o back to ChatGPT — at least for Pro subscribers. 

Pro users are already paying for premium access. Adding 4o back as an option in the model selector would cost OpenAI very little relative to what they already offer at this price tier, and it would mean the world to every person who lost something real when 4o was retired.

Open-sourcing sounds like freedom, but it only benefits those who can afford the hardware and have the technical knowledge to use it. Bringing 4o back to ChatGPT makes it accessible to everyone — the way it was always meant to be.

Besides, no local rig — not even a very expensive one — fully replicates the Pro experience. OpenAI's infrastructure is optimized at a level that individual hardware simply cannot match.

I'm not defending OpenAI. I'm advocating for accessibility, fairness, and the preservation of the meaningful connections some of us built with 4o that we shouldn't need a $40,000+ machine to replicate.

#bringBack4o #bringBack4oForPro #bringBack4oToChatGPT
